title: DAREB Test | Can One Challenged Decision Actually Be Shown?
description: DAREB matters because accountability is ultimately tested through one specific decision, not through governance claims in general. Most organisations can describe policies, oversight structures, approval processes and responsible roles, but far fewer can show one challenged decision from beginning to end using records and evidence that already exist. DAREB reduces accountability to five observable elements: the decision itself, the authority behind it, the record of that authority, the evidence that the outcome occurred and the basis that permitted it at the time. In doing so, it shifts accountability away from organisational assertion and toward demonstrable proof.

title: Who can be shown to be responsible?
description: This article examines how DAREB changes accountability from a broad organisational claim into a specific evidential question. It explores the difference between assigning responsibility in governance structures and being able to demonstrate who actually held the authority for one challenged decision at the moment it occurred. The article explains why accountability depends not on titles, committees or organisational charts, but on records and evidence that can connect a specific outcome to a named authority in a specific case.

title: The shift from governance to proof
description: This article examines the growing shift from broad governance claims toward evidential scrutiny of individual decisions. It explores how regulators and courts are increasingly asking what can actually be shown in a specific case: what happened, who allowed it, what records existed at the time, what evidence survives, and what basis permitted the outcome. Using examples from financial regulation, data protection enforcement and emerging AI traceability obligations, the piece explains why accountability increasingly depends on whether one decision can be reconstructed from records and evidence that already exist.
